(AbhW 3.3; Sum 28.14).

definition: ?) a part. kind of bird, swallow or swift (rock-swallow? cf SW 25.10). But it seems also to be a bird of prey; cf RL 1.72. For ModJ Pig. JNHW (s.v. ḍĕḍali) gives ``a kind of swallow''; GR gives (s.v. kĕḍali): ``kĕḍali, also ḍali, swallow''; (s.v. dĕdali): ``dĕdali, kawi = kĕḍali (ḍĕḍali)''; (s.v. ḍali): ``ḍali and ḍĕḍali, (poet.) vulture, a bird of prey''. See also dali-dali.

definition: (Skt that which is established or firm, law; usage, customary observance or prescribed conduct, duty; right, justice; virtue, morality, religion, good works; the law or doctrine of Buddhism; the forms or conditions of apparent reality; nature, character (= swabhāwa), essential quality, property, mark) 1. the rule of life and conduct, as established by divine disposition and laid down in religious law; one's pre-ordained course of life; code of duties. See also a-, kṣatriya-, lokika-, swa-, ugra-. 2. virtue, righteousness, piety, benevolence; (adj.) virtuous, righteous, pious, benevolent, charitable. See also alpa-, amita-, ati-, buddhi-, dāna-, parama-, puṇya-, su-. 3. (in Buddhism) the doctrine or truth, as preached by the Buddha. In Mahāyāna it is identified with the Absolute. See also iṣṭi-, paramārtha-, śūnya-. 4. nature, property 5. religious foundation, temple etc

106.6: ḍĕṅĕn iṅ triloka tumatiṅ haneṅ wulu nira;

9.15: harṣa ta saṅ mahāmuni tĕke ḍĕṅĕn sānak śiṣya nira kabeh.
(s.v. kaula) gives it as a synonym of kaula: ḍĕṅĕn patik watĕk punpunan bala pañjak;
(s.v. ḍĕṅĕn, Cnṭ.212): saḍĕṅĕnmu tĕkeṅ tuhanmu kon tumuluṅa;
(s.v. ḍĕṅĕn I, from a Wariga): ari-ari tumibā riṅ pṛthiwī mandadi ḍĕṅĕn mahābhūta, kaliṅanya: tatwawit ḍĕṅĕn sanak.

(s.v. sĕmpuluṅ) mentions the burying of the ari-ari (placenta) which is considered to become a spirit. It afflicts with illness if, e.g. while digging, one's shadow touches it. From the preceding the following conclusion might be warranted: The original meaning of ḍĕṅĕn is ``companion'' (cf. mal. dĕṅan, sund. deuṅeun). The ari-ari becomes an (evil) spirit, present in a person's shadow and so constantly accompanying him (ḍĕṅĕn III and II). Ḍĕṅĕn I could either be an ``accompanying'' shrine, or, more likely, a shrine connected with bhūta-wor#ship. Cf the CA passage, quoted in KBW (s.v. subhikṣa): sakweh niṅ kuśala dharma kuṭi sama kinon amahayu de śrī mahārāja, mwah winah (wineh?) pamuja ḍĕṅĕn.

1.99a: den sabuki limar. Passim. For the connection of den with (n)dan, q.v., the following is significant:

95.3b: sumambut musuhnyeka pi ndan tujah den cacah cūrṇa saṅsāra denya n kĕna ndan pirul dan kumis len ikaṅ daitya mohâṅa#muk dan dinuk dan gutuk riṅ watu ndan pupuh den wunuh len tinalyan rinantaynya pan den dudut denya kady āmatĕk den waweṅ ambara; cf 96.8: maṅkin krodha Mahoda#râmurĕkakĕn hastinya pan den wĕlĕg. If we assume that den = (n)dan, this would explain forms such as den cacah alongside ndan pupuh (with loss of infix -um-), without resort to a passive (cf ndak warah kita). On analogy with passive forms using denya, dera etc, den may then have become a passive prefix.

Although the use of diwya and dibya is mixed, there appears to be a preference for diwya when the meaning is ``divine'', ``supernatural'', and for dibya when it is more ``excellent'' etc in general. However, RY has only once diwya (2.22; astra diwya) as compared to dibya dozens of times. Diwya and dibya are very rare in kidungs.

2.48: kadrĕwinan kalih lali apuhara kaṅ woṅ yan riṅ uni sekagati (Sunda and Upasunda). Drawina etc in kidungs (and once KD) only. Skt drawina: movable property, goods, wealth, money. Gonda (SI p. 336) thinks that lit. Jav. ``banquet'' has its origin in this drawina, ``one of the aspects of wealth being a well-spread table''. It seems more likely that drawina, drinks (in kid.) is influenced by Skt drawa, ``liquid''. Cf Skt drawya (lex.), spirituous liquor.

26.29: pañjut ḍuḍuk (``sitting'', i.e. standing, not hanging, lamp). The connection with ḍuḍuk (to sit) of the following piḍuḍuk (in kid. piduduk) and its derivatives does not seem certain. Piḍuḍu#kan appears to be a preparatory ceremony, which precedes the principal one (wedding, initiation etc), and has a purificatory and (magically) strengthening function. The various requisites used for that purpose (clothes, offerings etc) are called piḍuḍuk or piḍuḍukan; cf wali, bali, pali.

(s.v. bali) gives kababalinin as bal. rendering of piniḍuḍuk, and explains bali with ``festival clothes, put away after the feast''. This meaning seems to be found in paragi piḍuḍuk and in pidudutan = piḍuḍukan (see under dudut III).

124.4: anulis ṭikā dinudutan puḍak. In RY the form dinudutakĕn is found four times. It seems difficult to connect it directly with aṅdudut, ``to pull''. 10.2: ūrṇâbhrā lumarap ya tâṅdyuti buṅah ni mukha nira.
12.4: bhaṭāra Pāwaka makāgra nikāṅ amara / kalawan ikaṅ watĕk dyuti diwākarawaktra sira;
13.10a: amisreṅ saṅ kawi huwus lwir jyuti kumawwat eṅ sasi.
